entry.title: Re: SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud | How to Replicate Worker/Us...
entry.content:
Dear @quocdt5,

There is no standard transport mechanism for moving worker or business-user master data directly from DEV to QAS in Public Cloud. These records are system-specific and must exist separately in each system

The appropriate procedure depends on how the data is managed:

1. Manually maintained workers

Import the same worker data separately into QAS using the CSV import functionality in the Manage Workforce app. The templates can be prepared with the same Worker IDs and Work Agreement IDs as in DEV, provided that these IDs do not already exist in QAS

2. Workers originating from an HR system

When SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central or another HR system is the leading source the worker data should be replicated independently to both DEV and QAS. SAP supports inbound workforce replication through SAP Master Data Integration and the corresponding workforce integration services

3. Business users

Business users should be created or provisioned separately in QAS. For central user management, SAP Cloud Identity Services - Identity Provisioning can provision business users and their business role assignments to Public Cloud. Note that the corresponding worker must already exist in QAS before the provisioning job can create the business user, and the required business roles must already exist in QAS before the assignments are provisioned, see KBA 3405942

4. Business roles

Business-role definitions can be transported from DEV to QAS using the standard IAM transport process. However, transporting a business role does not transport the corresponding worker or business-user master record

More details below:

  • SAP Help – How to Mass Import Worker Data
  • SAP Help – How to Fill In Downloaded Worker Templates
  • SAP Help – Workforce Inbound Replication
  • SAP Help – SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition as an Identity Provisioning Target
  • SAP Help – Transport Management for IAM Entities
  • SAP Help – How to Maintain Business Users
  • SAP KBA 3405942 - FAQs about Identity Provisioning Service in S/4HANA Public Cloud

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: PTP WM Supplier return form - SAP S4 HANA public cloud
entry.content:
MMIM_ITEM_PROVIDE_DATA:
This BAdI is valid for enriching item-level data during goods movement processing. It’s why you see your custom logic firing multiple times and values appearing in the trace. However, this enrichment is transient — unless the fields are part of the form’s interface, they won’t persist into the XML.

MMIM_FORM_DATA_ENRICHMENT:
This is the recommended BAdI when your goal is specifically to push data into the Adobe form output. It ensures that whatever you enrich is carried forward into the XML payload that the form template consumes. In other words, it’s the persistence layer for form output.

Having said that your logic in MMIM_ITEM_PROVIDE_DATA is correct, but the values won’t persist unless you expose them as custom fields in the form interface and bind them in the Adobe template. The recommended BAdI for form output enrichment is MMIM_FORM_DATA_ENRICHMENT, not MMIM_ITEM_PROVIDE_DATA.

entry.title: Enable barcode scanning for Product field (/SCWM/S_RF_SELECTION-RFPROD) in Stock/Bin query product
entry.content:
Hi All,

We have a requirement to enable GS1/EAN128 barcode scanning for the Product field in the RF "Stock/Bin Query by Product" transaction.

Expectation - To scan a  barcode string containing Product and Batch information into the Product field. The application should then decode the barcode and populate the Product and Batch fields.

Current Scenario - The Product field refers to /SCWM/S_RF_SELECTION-RFPROD which has conversion exit. When a barcode string is longer than 40 characters, the value is truncated before further processing.

I checked for notes but did not found anything relating this.
2863559 - Error /SCWM/RF_EN195 by RF "Stock / Bin Query by Product" product barcode scan - this was closer but not of much help.

Is there any SAP Note, enhancement, or recommended standard approach to support this requirement? If not, is the recommended solution to create a custom RF program/screen with a barcode input field (without a conversion exit).

Any guidance or references to relevant SAP Notes would be appreciated.

Thank you.

Regards,

Karthik

entry.title: 5 Fiori Apps Every Sales Key User Should Know in the O2C (SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition)
entry.content:
The Order-to-Cash (O2C) process is one of the most critical end-to-end processes in Sales. To efficiently manage customer orders, monitor deliveries, and create invoices on time, sales teams need the right tools. SAP Fiori provides a wide range of role-based applications that increase transparency and simplify daily sales operations.

Below are five Fiori apps that, in my opinion, every Sales Key User in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition should know.

My Sales Overview (App ID F2200)

My Sales Overview is the ideal starting point for sales representatives. The app consolidates key information such as open sales orders, quotations, returns, and other sales-related KPIs in one central dashboard. This enables users to identify priorities more quickly and jump directly into the most relevant business processes.

SAP Help Portal:
My Sales Overview | SAP Help Portal

Manage Sales Orders (App ID F1873)

Manage Sales Orders is one of the most important applications in daily sales operations. It enables users to search, filter, display, and edit customer orders. In addition, it provides direct access to document flow and related follow-on processes.

Benefits

Fast search for customer orders

Transparency into current order status

Direct navigation to follow-on documents

Efficient processing of open orders

SAP Help Portal:
Manage Sales Orders | SAP Help Portal

Track Sales Orders (App ID F2577)

Today’s customers expect immediate visibility into the status of their orders. With Track Sales Orders, sales representatives can monitor the current processing status of an order and determine whether it has already been delivered, billed, or is still in process.

Benefits

  • End-to-end visibility of customer orders
  • Transparency into delivery and billing status
  • Faster response times to customer inquiries
  • Insight into the complete document flow

SAP Help Portal:
Track Sales Orders | SAP Help Portal

Sales Order Fulfillment – Analyze and Resolve Issues (App ID F0029A)

Within the O2C process, issues frequently arise that can prevent timely delivery or billing. The Sales Order Fulfillment – Analyze and Resolve Issues app helps users proactively identify and resolve such obstacles. Examples include credit blocks, missing information, or supply shortages.

Benefits

  • Early identification of critical process issues
  • Analysis of fulfillment risks
  • Faster issue resolution
  • Improved delivery performance and customer satisfaction

SAP Help Portal:
Sales Order Fulfillment - Analyze and Resolve Issues | SAP Help Portal

Manage Billing Documents (App ID F0797 / App ID F0797A)

Billing represents the final step in the Order-to-Cash process. With Manage Billing Documents (App ID F0797) and its successor Manage Billing Documents – Version 2 (App ID F0797A), invoices, credit memos, and other billing documents can be managed centrally. Users can display, edit, post, and cancel billing documents while maintaining full visibility of the billing process.

Benefits

  • Centralized management of billing documents
  • Transparency throughout the billing process
  • Faster invoice processing
  • Support for timely revenue recognition

SAP Help Portal:

  • App ID F0797: Manage Billing Documents | SAP Help Portal
  • App ID F0797A: Manage Billing Documents - Version 2 | SAP Help Portal

Conclusion

SAP Fiori provides powerful applications that support the entire Order-to-Cash process. In particular, My Sales Overview (App ID F2200), Manage Sales Orders (App ID F1873), Track Sales Orders (App ID F2577), Sales Order Fulfillment – Analyze and Resolve Issues (App ID F0029A), and Manage Billing Documents (App ID F0797/App ID F0797A) help organizations increase transparency, identify issues earlier, and improve efficiency in daily sales operations.

entry.title: SAP Commerce Cloud with Composable Storefront and SmartEdit: Config Notes from a Demo Server Upgrade
entry.content:
The Problem

Recently, I was upgrading a demo instance of SAP Commerce Cloud on the public cloud environment (CCv2), including the Composable Storefront (Spartacus) and SmartEdit. On paper, everything looked fine - the build succeeded, the deployment finished green, and all the endpoints returned 200.

But when I actually tried to use the system, things started falling apart:

  • 🚫The Composable Storefront wouldn’t load properly: OCC calls were failing and login flows were broken.
  • 🚫 Only one storefront was reachable: I could visit either the B2C or the B2B URL, but not both at the same time.
  • 🚫 SmartEdit login didn’t work: after clicking "Sign In", I was thrown into an error.
  • 🚫Once inside SmartEdit, the storefront preview was blank — an empty iframe staring back at me, with no obvious errors in the UI.

After quite some digging through browser dev tools, Backoffice properties, and the CCv2 Cloud Portal, I realized the root cause wasn’t one big issue - it was a series of small configuration details, spread across Spartacus, Backoffice, and the CCv2 portal, that each needed to be exactly right.

To save others the same headache, I’ve collected my notes here in one place. If you’re doing a similar upgrade or deployment, this checklist should help you get things running smoothly.

1. Composable Storefront Configuration

There are a few things to review inside your composable storefront app:

1.1 manifest.json in js-storefront - Declare Both B2C and B2B Storefronts

If you want to run both a B2C and a B2B Composable Storefront within the same environment, both apps must be declared in the applications section of the Spartacus manifest.json in the js-storefront folder. Without both entries, only one storefront will be built and deployed - which is exactly what caused the "only one storefront is reachable" issue in my case:

{
  "applications": [
    {
      "name": "b2cstore",
      "path": "b2cstore",
      "ssr": {
        "enabled": true,
        "path": "dist/b2cstore/server/server.mjs"
      },
      "csr": {
        "webroot": "dist/b2cstore/browser/"
      },
      "enabledRepositories": ["spartacus-dev"]
    },
    {
      "name": "b2bstore",
      "path": "b2bstore",
      "ssr": {
        "enabled": true,
        "path": "dist/b2bstore/server/server.mjs"
      },
      "csr": {
        "webroot": "dist/b2bstore/browser/"
      },
      "enabledRepositories": ["spartacus-dev"]
    }
  ],
  "nodeVersion": "22"
}

Make sure:

  • this is updated before the build, since the manifest drives what gets packaged and deployed.
  • to adjust the name, path, ssr/csr settings accordingly. The names will be used later in a property configuration.
  • more information in Add Applications to JavaScript Storefronts

1.2 spartacus-configuration.module.ts

Make sure the OCC endpoint and prefix are aligned with your CCv2 backend, e.g.: 

backend: {
  occ: {
    baseUrl: 'https://api.<your-env>.model-t.cc.commerce.ondemand.com',
    prefix: '/occ/v2/',
  },
},

Note:

  • if you have more than one storefronts, adjust both spartacus-configuration.module.ts files.
  • you can also use a place holder for the baseUrl, e.g. OCC_BACKEND_BASE_URL_VALUE, just remember to also adjust index.html and the related property value on Cloud Portal, details cf. the step 10 in Add Applications to JavaScript Storefronts.

1.3 smart-edit-feature.module.ts

The allowOrigin property must include the domain hosting SmartEdit (typically your Backoffice URL). Without this, SmartEdit will not be able to communicate with your storefront inside the iframe, e.g.: 

smartEdit: {
  storefrontPreviewRoute: 'cx-preview',
  allowOrigin: 'backoffice.<your-env>.model-t.cc.commerce.ondemand.com:443',
},

Don’t forget the port (usually :443) — SmartEdit is strict about the origin format.

2. SAP Commerce Cloud Configuration

2.1 manifest.json in core-customize

Before triggering a build in CCv2, make sure your Commerce manifest.json in the core-customize folder has the correct webapp entries.

Both the backoffice and app entries under webapps need authorizationserver correctly registered:

"aspects": [
   {
   "name": "backoffice",
   "webapps": [
                ...,
                {
                    "name": "authorizationserver",
                    "contextPath": "/authorizationserver"
                },
                ...
   ,{
   "name": "api",
   "webapps": [
                ...,
                {
                    "name": "authorizationserver",
                    "contextPath": "/authorizationserver"
                },
                ...

This ensures the auth server is reachable at the expected path in the public cloud environment. Why this is important to point out? The oauth2 extension was previously used as the name for the JDK 17 version. However, with the JDK 21 version of Commerce Cloud, the authorizationserver extension should be used accordingly.

2.2 Map Each Storefront to Its Hostname (JS Storefront Service)

Declaring both apps in the Spartacus manifest.json is only half the story, CCv2 also needs to know which hostname routes to which storefront. Otherwise, requests to both b2cjsstorefront... and b2bjsstorefront... may end up hitting the wrong app (or none at all) - again, exactly the "only one storefront reachable" symptom.

Add the following properties to the JS Storefront service in the CCv2 Cloud Portal:

jsapps.b2cstore.servername.1=b2cjsstorefront.<your-env>.model-t.cc.commerce.ondemand.com
jsapps.b2bstore.servername.1=b2bjsstorefront.<your-env>.model-t.cc.commerce.ondemand.com

The property key uses the app name from the Spartacus manifest.json (b2cstore / b2bstore), and the value is the hostname that should route to that app.

2.3 OAuth Client Setup

2.3.1 Basic Setup

If it doesn’t already exist, create the appropriate OAuth Client in Backoffice with the name mobile_android_public , which is used by the Composable Storefront by default, with the following properties:

  • Password: none
  • Authorities: ROLE_CLIENT
  • Authorized grant types: refresh_token, authorization_code
  • Scope: basic
  • OAuth registered redirect URIs — must contain all storefront hosts, for example:
    • https://b2cjsstorefront.<your-env>.model-t.cc.commerce.ondemand.com
    • https://b2bjsstorefront.<your-env>.model-t.cc.commerce.ondemand.com
  • Custom login page URI:
    https://{redirectUriHost}/login
    The {redirectUriHost} placeholder gets resolved dynamically based on the redirect URI being used.

2.3.2 Allowed Hosts for the Custom Login Page

In hcs_common service in the CCv2 Cloud Portal, set:

authserver.oauthclientdetails.loginpageuri.allowed.hosts=b2cjsstorefront.<host>,b2bjsstorefront.<host>

Without this property, the custom login page URI won’t resolve to the storefront hosts and login flows will break.

3. Getting SmartEdit to Work

3.1 OAuth Client Setup

In Backoffice, find thesmartedit OAuth Client, make sure the registered redirect URI must point to the SmartEdit URL on Backoffice:

https://backoffice.<your-env>.model-t.cc.commerce.ondemand.com/smartedit

3.2 Update the Site’s Preview URL

In Backoffice, under WCMS/Website node, the related CMS Site’s Preview URL (for either B2C or B2B site, under ) must point to your real Composable Storefront, for example in the default electronics-spa site (B2C electronics demo storefront):

https://b2cjsstorefront.<your-env>.model-t.cc.commerce.ondemand.com/electronics-spa/en/USD/

This is what SmartEdit loads inside its iframe.

3.3 Adjust the Content-Security-Policy

SmartEdit needs to embed the storefront in an IFrame. In the hcs_common service in CCV2 (or in the related properties file), adjust:

smartedit.response.header.Content-Security-Policy=... frame-src 'self' ... https://b2cjsstorefront.<your-env>.model-t.cc.commerce.ondemand.com https://b2bjsstorefront.<your-env>.model-t.cc.commerce.ondemand.com

This tells the browser to allow the storefront to be framed from the SmartEdit host.

3.4 Unset X-Frame-Options via HTTP Response Header Sets

By default, CCv2 sets X-Frame-Options: DENY (or SAMEORIGIN), which will completely block SmartEdit’s iframe, regardless of your CSP settings.

For demo purpose, you can use create a new HTTP Response Header Set in the Security node in the CCv2 Cloud Portal to unset X-Frame-Options 

and apply it to the affected endpoint, such as the B2C or B2B storefront endpoint. Modern browsers will then fall back to the Content-Security-Policy frame-ancestors directive.

Wrap-Up Checklist ✅

Here’s a quick summary of everything to verify, roughly in the order you should tackle them:

  • [ ] Spartacus manifest.json — both b2cstore and b2bstore declared in applications (before the build!)
  • [ ] Commerce manifest.json — authorizationserver webapp registered in both backoffice and app (before the build!)
  • [ ] spartacus-configuration.module.ts — OCC baseUrl and prefix correct (before the build!)
  • [ ] smart-edit-feature.module.ts — allowOrigin includes SmartEdit host (before the build!)
  • [ ] JS Storefront service — jsapps.<appname>.servername.0 properties set for each storefront
  • [ ] OAuth client mobile_android_public created with proper grants, scope, and redirect URIs
  • [ ] Custom login page URI configured with {redirectUriHost} placeholder
  • [ ] authserver.oauthclientdetails.loginpageuri.allowed.hosts set in hcs_common
  • [ ] OAuth client smartedit has correct redirect URI
  • [ ] SmartEdit: site preview URL updated to Composable Storefront
  • [ ] smartedit.response.header.Content-Security-Policy set in hcs_common with correct frame-src
  • [ ] X-Frame-Options unset via HTTP Response Header Sets in CCv2 portal

Final Thoughts

Individually, these are all small changes - but miss just one and you’ll end up staring at a blank SmartEdit iframe, chasing a login redirect loop, wondering why only one of your storefronts is reachable, or debugging a CORS error you can’t quite pin down. Hopefully this checklist saves you a few debugging hours.

If you’re planning a similar upgrade or migration, my advice is: treat manifest.json (both Spartacus and Commerce), OAuth client configuration, CSP headers, and X-Frame-Options as first-class citizens during your deployment - they’re the most common source of "why isn’t this working?" moments.

entry.title: OData URL Decode Strategy (or delete duplicate $skiptoken)
entry.content:
Dear SAP Community,

There is this problem where the $skiptoken is being incremented but not being cleared. 

Run sequence 1 --> ?$skiptoken=100
Run sequence 2 --> ?$skiptoken=100&$skiptoken=200
Run sequence 3 --> ?$skiptoken=100&$skiptoken=200&$skiptoken=300
Run sequence 4 --> ?$skiptoken=100&$skiptoken=200&$skiptoken=300&$skiptoken=400

This only happens when the the client queries multiple page. If there are more records, there is a *next* link containing the URL of the next page. Now, the URL being returned is encoded and has multiple $skiptoken each time it is run (as shown above).

This is true regardless if we run from SAP Gateway Client, Postman, web browser, and Workato (which is the actual production client).

To better demonstrate, please see below screen.

May I ask if there is a SAP standard solution for this? or if not, at least custom ABAP code in my web service. I tried to debug but the skiptoken only contains a numeric value. 

Because right now, my proposal is that the Workato peeps should do the URL decoding before using it for the next page. (Not quite sure if they are amenable to that)

entry.title: Re: EBRR S/4HANA Public Cloud — WIP Clearing Zero Invoice...
entry.content:
Hello,

 

Your question doesn't describe: 

  • Which integration are you using — CPM projects, sales orders, service documents, or another?
  • Which revenue recognition key and recognition method is configured for your scenario?

Let me describe general EBRR behaviour:

Real-time posting date: EBRR always posts the subsequent real-time document with the same posting date as the originating (prima nota) document. If a billing document is created on 2 July with a backdated posting date of 30 June, the EBRR real-time document is also posted in June. 

Period-end close jobs: EBRR period-end close jobs can be run as many times as needed within the same period. Each run considers all documents posted up to the end of the selected fiscal period — there is no concept of a "final" run after which further documents are excluded. If a billing document is posted after the last period-end run, simply schedule the job again for the same period and it will be picked up. If the job is not re-run, the document will be processed in the next period.

No blocking of business processes: EBRR does not set any flag after a period-end run, nor does it generate warnings or blocks when additional documents are posted in an already-processed period. EBRR is designed not to interrupt any operational business process in real time. If you want to prevent backdated postings after a certain point, the correct approach is to close the FI posting period

If  you believe the system is behaving incorrectly or unexpectedly, we encourage you to raise a customer incident with SAP Support. Please include document numbers, posting dates, and the revenue recognition key in use — this will allow the support team to investigate the exact system behaviour in your landscape.

If the functionality doesn't meet your business requirements — for example, if you would like EBRR to provide a period-end lock mechanism or additional warnings for backdated postings — submit a Customer Influence (CIP) request at influence.sap.com. CIP requests allow the product team to evaluate new functionality based on customer demand and prioritise it in future releases.

 

Best regards,

Petr

entry.title: Re: FBL1N - Order Number selection criterion no longer fi...
entry.content:
Hi Raymond,
 
Thank you for the suggested notes.
 
I reviewed both SAP Note 310886 and KBA 3455167. As far as I understand, they mainly address issues related to Dynamic Selections and custom fields added through the logical database (KDF) using transaction SE36.
 
In our case, the issue seems slightly different:
- The "Order Number" field is already available in FBL1N.
- The value can be entered without any issue.
- However, in S/4HANA the report output is not filtered according to the entered value.
- Using the same selection criteria in an ECC system, the filtering works as expected.
 
Therefore, the problem does not appear to be related to missing dynamic selection fields or SE36 customization, but rather to the way the selection criterion is processed during report execution in S/4HANA.
 
I will continue investigating and may raise an SAP support case to determine whether this is an intended S/4HANA behavior, a known issue, or a missing correction.
 
Thank you again for your help and suggestions.
 
Best regards,
Florent

entry.title: Re: SAP S/4HANA BP screen enhancement with custom tab
entry.content:
I had exactly the same issue. Since I couldn't find many posts describing this specific situation, I wanted to share the solution here in case it helps someone else in the future. This thread was extremely helpful in pointing me in the right direction.

After some debugging, I discovered that the problem was not in the BDT implementation itself, but in the CVI extension structures.

Initially, I had extended the following structures:

CVIS_EI_EXTERN
  -> CUSTOMER
    -> CENTRAL_DATA
      -> CENTRAL
        -> DATA
        -> DATAX

However, my custom fields were appended to KNVV (Sales Area Data). For KNVV fields, this is not the correct location.

The custom fields must instead be added to:

CVIS_EI_EXTERN
  -> CUSTOMER
    -> SALES_DATA
      -> SALES
        -> DATA
        -> DATAX

Once I added my Z-fields to both the DATA and DATAX structures under SALES_DATA, the values were correctly transferred by the CVI framework and persisted in the KNVV table during BP save.

So, if your custom KNVV fields are displayed correctly in the BP screen but are not saved to the database, double-check that you have extended the Sales Data structures instead of the Central Data structures. This small detail was the root cause of my issue.

Hopefully this saves someone else a few hours of debugging!

entry.title: Re: SAP QM - How to Perform Partial Reversal of Usage Dec...
entry.content:
When you complete a UD, the system posts the entire accepted/rejected quantity from inspection stock to the target stock type (e.g., 321 → unrestricted). SAP standard supports complete reversal of the UD posting (movement type 322), but not partial reversal. The reversal is tied to the inspection lot status. Once UD is made, the lot is considered closed, and SAP does not allow you to “re‑open” it for partial corrections.

Hence, Partial reversal is possible via BAPI_GOODSMVT_CREATE with 322, but the inspection lot quantities/status will not be updated. Best practice is to treat the partial reversal as a stock correction, not a QM correction. The UD remains valid, and you avoid touching QM tables directly.

entry.title: The Missing Web Search: Bolting Brave onto an OpenClaw Agent
entry.content:
The Missing Web Search: Bolting Brave onto an OpenClaw Agent

As of 2026-07-10. Config snippets and CLI versions reflect the state of OpenClaw and the bx CLI at time of writing. TOOLS.md excerpts contain internal timestamps (2026-07-07/08) reflecting when that config was written — not the post date.

A small OpenClaw story about a real gap in the default tool lineup, and the surprisingly durable workaround: drop a binary on persistent storage, prepend $PATH, and let the agent shell out.

TL;DR

The problem:

  • My LLM agent framework of choice (OpenClaw) ships 13 web-search provider integrations.
  • Twelve of them need an API key.
  • The one that doesn't — DuckDuckGo — is currently bot-detection-blocked for programmatic use, so web_search returns empty results or 403s in practice.
  • And routing Brave / Tavily / Exa / etc. through the LiteLLM proxy in front of SAP AI Core isn't the answer either — search APIs authenticate per-caller, not per-model-request. That's a structural limitation of every LLM proxy, not an OpenClaw quirk.

The solution:

  • Install the Brave Search CLI (bx) as a static binary into your agent's workspace, add that path to tools.exec.pathPrepend, and let the agent invoke it via exec.
  • Tell the agent about it in TOOLS.md.
  • ✅Survives container rebuilds (the workspace is a persistent volume).
  • ✅Works with any model — the search key never touches your LLM proxy.
  • ✅One binary drop, one config line, one TOOLS.md note. ~20 minutes end-to-end.
  • ✅Brave's free tier includes $5/month in credits — light agent usage fits entirely inside that.

Note: If you run an OpenClaw agent behind LiteLLM (or any other model proxy) and don't want to plumb search-API keys into your model provider, this is the pattern.

The gap

Here's the tools block from my live agent config, verbatim:

tools:
  web:
    search:
      enabled: true
      provider: duckduckgo
      maxResults: 10
      timeoutSeconds: 30
      cacheTtlMinutes: 15
  exec:
    pathPrepend:
      - /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/bin
 

✅Eight lines. Two moving parts. That's the whole story of this post.

The tools.web.search block enables the built-in web_search tool the agent sees. Provider duckduckgo was chosen for one reason: it doesn't require an API key. Everything else in the search provider lineup does:

 

ProviderShips asNeeds key?
DuckDuckGoduckduckgo-searchNo (public HTML scrape)
Bravebrave-searchYes
TavilytavilyYes
Exaexa-searchYes
SearXNGsearxng-searchDepends on your instance
Perplexityperplexity-searchYes
Geminigemini-searchYes (Google AI)
Grokgrok-searchYes (xAI)
Kimikimi-searchYes
MiniMaxminimax-searchYes
Ollamaollama-searchLocal runtime
Parallelparallel-searchYes
FirecrawlfirecrawlYes

For a proof-of-concept in a fresh workspace, DuckDuckGo is the frictionless choice. In production, it's the only keyless option — and it turns out to be unreliable enough that I stopped using it.

Why not just add a search key to the LiteLLM config?

The obvious question if you already run LiteLLM in front of SAP AI Core: can't I plumb a Brave/Tavily/Exa key through the same proxy that fronts my models?

Short answer: no.

  • LiteLLM (and every other model proxy — LangSmith proxy, LiteLLM Gateway, custom internal ones) is a model proxy.
  • It translates OpenAI-compatible chat/completions calls to whatever backend serves them.
  • It has no concept of "search tools" as first-class routable endpoints, because search APIs aren't a chat/completions verb — they're separate, per-caller-authenticated services.

You can, in theory, wrap a search API in a custom proxy plugin. But now you're maintaining a fork, and the proxy is doing something outside its job description. Not worth it for one CLI-shaped problem.

The other alternative is model-native web search — the search happens inside the model's tenancy, on the provider's side.

It works, but:

  • It only works for a subset of models (not the Claude tiers I route through SAP AI Core).
  • Results and cost are opaque.
  • You lose the ability to grep, cache, filter, or otherwise inspect raw search results.

I wanted a search path that:

  • Works with any model I route through LiteLLM.
  • Costs money against a search vendor I chose, on a bill I control.
  • Returns structured JSON I can pipe through jq and reason over.

That means: a CLI, invoked via exec, using a search-vendor API key held in a workspace env var.

web_search vs. web_fetch — don't confuse them

Two built-in tools that are easy to mix up:

Tool Input Output When you'd use it

web_searchA query stringRanked list of results (titles, URLs, snippets) — no full page contentYou don't know which page has the answer; you want candidates.
web_fetchA specific URLReadable text extracted from that one pageYou already have the URL; you want its content.

web_fetch is not affected by the DuckDuckGo problem — it's a URL-to-content extractor and it works fine. But it can't find pages for you. If your agent needs to discover a URL first, it needs web_search — exactly the tool that's broken.

This is also why bx context is a nice primitive: it does both jobs in a single API call. Search + extract, one round-trip, one budget.

In the built-in tools that would be web_search → pick a URL → web_fetch (two round-trips), assuming web_search returned anything useful.

DuckDuckGo: why the default failed in practice

OpenClaw's duckduckgo-search provider is a lightweight HTML scrape of duckduckgo.com.

It works well right up until DuckDuckGo decides you look like a bot — which happens fast when an autonomous agent fires three queries a minute from a datacenter IP.

Symptoms:

  • Empty results: [] arrays for queries that clearly have results in a browser.
  • Occasional HTTP 403 with anti-abuse messaging in the body.
  • Intermittent "success" responses containing a CAPTCHA challenge as the first "result."

None of this is a bug in OpenClaw. It's the reality of scraping a search UI that doesn't want to be scraped.

The DuckDuckGo Instant Answer API is a real API but returns almost nothing useful for open-ended queries — it's designed for unit conversions and Wikipedia summaries.

⚠️ The worst kind of tool is one that looks like it works. Silent failure at the tool layer produces silent hallucination at the model layer — the agent confidently reports "no results found" when in fact the search never ran.

The workspace-binary pattern

A little context on where this agent runs:

OpenClaw is deployed on the same Kubernetes cluster as the diagnostic agent from the companion post. It runs as a long-lived sandbox (a pod) with a PersistentVolumeClaim mounted at /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/. Model traffic goes through the same LiteLLM proxy that fronts SAP AI Core — Claude Sonnet/Opus tiers, Nova, embeddings — all on the sap/* catalog.

Everything under ~/.openclaw/workspace/ is durable state. Everything outside it (system packages, /tmp, npm globals) is ephemeral.

Combine that with tools.exec.pathPrepend, which injects arbitrary directories at the front of the agent's shell PATH, and you get a durable tool-extension pattern:

  1. Drop a static binary into ~/.openclaw/workspace/bin/.
  2. Add that directory to tools.exec.pathPrepend.
  3. Tell the agent (in TOOLS.md) when to reach for it.

No skill packaging, no plugin manifest, no framework code change.

  • ✅Survives container rebuilds.
  • ✅No custom operator, no sidecar.
  • ✅Hot-reloads — no agent restart.

Here's the current state on my workspace:

$ ls -la /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/bin/
-rwxrwxr-x 1 node node 2110240 Jul  7 18:15 bx

$ /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/bin/bx --version
bx 1.5.0
 

2.1 MB, a single self-contained Rust binary from the Brave team. Sitting there since 2026-07-07, survived several pod restarts.

The one-time config edit:

tools:
  exec:
    pathPrepend:
      - /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/bin
 

That is the entire mechanical change. Hot-reloads, no restart required.

This pattern isn't Brave-specific. Anything that ships as a static binary, needs an API key you don't want in your LLM proxy, and returns useful text/JSON on stdout is a candidate: yt-dlp, ripgrep, a custom internal CLI, whatever.

Installing bx durably

⚠️ Install into the persistent workspace, not the container's global path. Otherwise the binary vanishes on the next pod restart.

Option A — one-off manual install (from inside the agent sandbox or via exec😞

mkdir -p /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/bin
cd /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/bin

BX_VERSION=1.5.0
curl -sSL \
  "https://github.com/brave/brave-search-cli/releases/download/v${BX_VERSION}/bx-linux-x86_64" \
  -o bx
chmod +x bx

# Configure the API key once:
./bx config set-key "$BRAVE_SEARCH_API_KEY"

# Sanity check:
./bx --version
./bx web "kubernetes ingress rewrite" | head -20
 

Option B — Kubernetes initContainer (production-grade, automatic on pod start, arch-aware):

- name: bx-install
  image: curlimages/curl:latest
  command:
    - /bin/sh
    - -c
    - |
      mkdir -p /workspace/bin
      if [ ! -f /workspace/bin/bx ]; then
        ARCH=$(uname -m)
        [ "$ARCH" = "aarch64" ] && BX_ARCH="linux-arm64" || BX_ARCH="linux-amd64"
        curl -sL \
          "https://github.com/brave/brave-search-cli/releases/download/v1.5.0/bx-1.5.0-$BX_ARCH" \
          -o /workspace/bin/bx \
          && chmod 0755 /workspace/bin/bx \
          && echo "bx installed ($ARCH)" \
          || echo "bx install failed"
      else
        echo "bx already present: $(/workspace/bin/bx --version 2>/dev/null || echo unknown)"
      fi
  volumeMounts:
    - mountPath: /workspace
      name: workspaces-pvc
 

Option B is what we use in production. The if [ ! -f ... ] check makes it idempotent — on warm restarts it logs the version and exits immediately. BRAVE_SEARCH_API_KEY is injected from a Kubernetes Secret via envFrom.

💡 bx "query" (bare invocation) is an alias for bx context — both call the RAG-optimised extraction endpoint. The distinction matters when you're writing TOOLS.md, because the subcommand name signals intent to the agent.

The honest part: the agent doesn't discover this on its own

The model doesn't spontaneously pick bx over web_search. From the agent's perspective, web_search is a first-class, well-documented tool in the system prompt. bx is just an executable on $PATH, indistinguishable from curl or awk. The agent has no way to know that web_search returns garbage unless you tell it.

In OpenClaw, you tell it via TOOLS.md — a workspace-local file the agent is instructed to read for environment-specific tool notes. Here's the relevant section from mine:

## Web Search

**Preferred: `bx` (Brave Search CLI)** — the configured `web_search`
tool routes through DuckDuckGo which is currently bot-detection-blocked,
so use the `bx` CLI directly instead.

- Binary: `/home/node/.openclaw/workspace/bin/bx`
  (also on PATH via `tools.exec.pathPrepend`)
- API key: auto-loads from `BRAVE_SEARCH_API_KEY` env var
- Docs: https://brave.com/search/api/guides/use-with-openclaw/

**Which subcommand to pick:**
- `bx "query"` or `bx context "query"` → **default for RAG grounding**,
  pre-extracted clean text, token-budgeted. Best when reasoning over content.
- `bx answers "query"` → AI-synthesized answer with citations.
  ⚠️ **Not on current plan** — returns 400 `OPTION_NOT_IN_PLAN`.
  Don't call.
- `bx web "query"` → raw web results (titles/URLs/descriptions).
  Best for finding sources or scanning multiple.
- `bx news "query" --freshness pd|pw|pm|py` → news with time filter.
- `bx images`, `bx videos`, `bx places` → specialised.

**Example (raw web + jq):**
    bx web "world cup 2026 quarter finals" | \
      jq -r '.web.results[] | "\(.title)\n  \(.url)\n"'
 

Two things worth calling out:

It documents a plan-specific gotcha. bx answers returns OPTION_NOT_IN_PLAN on my current Brave tier. Without the note, the agent would call it, see the 400, and either loop or fall back to something worse. With the note, the agent skips it entirely. This is exactly the kind of environment-specific state that belongs in TOOLS.md, not in a shared skill.

It gives the agent a decision matrix. bx context and bx web return different shapes — context is pre-extracted clean text (perfect for feeding to a reasoning model), web is raw ranked results (perfect for scanning URLs). Documenting both means the agent doesn't have to guess. Writing just "Preferred: bx" would have it pick one subcommand and stick with it forever.

📝Your agent framework probably has an equivalent to TOOLS.md — whatever it's called (AGENTS.md, .claude.md, a system-prompt fragment, a project-specific tool doc), use it. This is where operational reality lives.

What bx context looks like in practice

$ bx "kubernetes ingress path rewrite rules" | jq '.grounding.generic[0]'
{
  "url": "https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/ingress/",
  "title": "Ingress | Kubernetes",
  "snippets": [
    "An Ingress may be configured to give Services externally-reachable URLs...",
    "The rewrite-target annotation lets you rewrite the path before forwarding..."
  ]
}
 

Three things worth noting:

  • Pre-extracted clean text. No HTML tags, no navigation cruft, no cookie banners. The snippets[] array is ready to inject into a prompt.
  • Structured JSON on stdout. Easy to filter, cache, and reason over programmatically.
  • Search + extract in one call. In the built-in tools that would be web_search → pick a URL → web_fetch — two round-trips. bx context collapses that to one.

Why this matters — how I extended Lulu's tool surface when built-in tools fall short

Both the Goose in-cluster diagnostic pipeline (from the companion post) and Lulu — my OpenClaw personal-assistant agent — share the same three foundations:

  • A Kubernetes runtime. A pod with a PersistentVolumeClaim, restarted freely, state preserved.
  • LiteLLM in front of SAP AI Core as the model gateway. All model traffic — Claude tiers, Nova, embeddings — routes through it.
  • A clean separation between what belongs in the LLM proxy and what doesn't. Model requests: through LiteLLM. Everything else: local to the agent's workspace.

The LLM proxy is a model router, not a general-purpose tool router. Trying to squeeze search APIs into it inverts the abstraction. The right home for those is the workspace PVC, invoked via exec, with keys held in workspace-local env vars.

Practical implications for anyone running OpenClaw (or a similar agent) on Kubernetes with SAP AI Core underneath:

  • ✅The LLM proxy is not the place for search. Don't try to plumb Brave/Tavily/Exa API keys through LiteLLM.
  • ✅The workspace PVC is the place for out-of-band tools. Anything that needs an API key that isn't a model provider belongs as a workspace-scoped CLI, invoked via exec, with the key held locally.
  • ✅ TOOLS.md is the place to teach the agent about them. Skills are for shared patterns; TOOLS.md is for your environment and your PVC.
  • ✅The Kubernetes primitives you already have are enough. A PVC, a static binary, and one tools.exec.pathPrepend line.

Takeaways

  1. OpenClaw's built-in web search relies on DuckDuckGo by default. Check what provider is configured in your tools.web.search block — it may be silently returning nothing useful.
  2. You can't route search-vendor API keys through an LLM proxy. Structural limitation. Don't fight it.
  3. On Kubernetes, the workspace PVC + tools.exec.pathPrepend is a durable tool-extension pattern. Any static binary works. bx is one example.
  4. The agent doesn't discover local tools on its own. TOOLS.md is how you tell it.
  5. Document plan-specific gotchas explicitly. The OPTION_NOT_IN_PLAN note saves the agent one failed round-trip every time it considers bx answers.
  6. Same runtime, different agent. The Goose diagnostic pipeline and the OpenClaw personal-assistant agent share the same cluster, the same LiteLLM/SAP AI Core plumbing, and the same PVC-as-scratch-disk pattern. That's a platform doing its job.
  • ✅Survives container rebuilds.
  • ✅ Works with any model routed through LiteLLM.
  • ✅ Fully auditable — the CLI runs in your workspace, on your bill.
  • ✅ Generalises to any tool that ships as a static binary.

References

 

WhatWhere
OpenClawhttps://openclaw.ai/ · https://docs.openclaw.ai/
Brave Search CLI (bx)https://github.com/brave/brave-search-cli
Brave × OpenClaw guidehttps://brave.com/search/api/guides/use-with-openclaw/
Brave Search API dashboardhttps://api-dashboard.search.brave.com/
OpenClaw config: tools.exec.pathPrependdocs.openclaw.ai → Gateway → Configuration Reference
OpenClaw config: tools.web.search.providerdocs.openclaw.ai → Tools → Web Search
SAP AI Corehttps://help.sap.com/docs/sap-ai-core
LiteLLMhttps://docs.litellm.ai/
Companion post: Kyma Unplugged diagnosticshttps://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/autonomous-diagnostics-on-kyma-unplugged-goose-litellm-kro-sap-ai-core-in/ba-p/14435287

Stack referenced: OpenClaw · Brave Search API · bx CLI · Kyma Unplugged · LiteLLM · SAP AI Core · KRO

entry.title: Re: CRM Case partner table
entry.content: Hi,Logic to Fetch the Partner Number:- 1.Pass the case id in the EXT_KEY in the table-CRMS4_CASESEARCH and get the Case Guid 2.excecute the function module se37 -BAPI_CRMCASE_GETDETAIL 3.Pass the Case Guid in GUID field under Import Parameter tab and click on excute button 4.Result screen page will be display. 5.Go to the ELEMENT_ATTRIBUTE result page 6.Get the VALUE and ELEM_NO against the all PARTNER_FCT under the name column. 7.Back from the screen and go to ELEMENT RESULT page. 8.Check the element number which are fetch from ELEMENT_ATTRIBUTE with BOR_TYPE #BUS1006,fetch the value of BOR_KEY 9.Like this you can get the partner involved in case id.

entry.title: The GTT Route Simulator in SAP Build
entry.content:
About a year ago I published a blog post describing an integrated container scenario built with several SAP solutions—SAP Transportation Management, BN4L Freight Collaboration, and BN4L Global Track & Trace (GTT)—all running in the Industry Template Plus environment. To make that scenario feel a bit more realistic, I created a small SAP Build application that simulates an ocean-liner’s tracking information: it sends events to GTT so that an overseas voyage can be “played back” in just five minutes.

Using this tool—aptly named the GTT Route Simulator—the journey from the Port of Guangzhou to the Port of Rotterdam consists of:

  1. Proof-of-pickup, loading-start, loading-end and departure events are reported in Guangzhou.
  2. Location-update events are sent every few seconds while the vessel is at sea.
  3. Upon arrival in Rotterdam, arrival, unloading-start, unloading-end and proof-of-delivery events are reported.

When the simulator finishes, the shipment tracked event log in Global Track & Trace resembles a real shipment history, only compressed in time:

Building the Route Simulator with SAP Build

I put the first version together in a very short time—mainly to explore SAP Build’s capabilities. For anyone new to it, SAP Build is SAP’s low-code portfolio on SAP Business Technology Platform that lets business users and developers:

  • Create apps (SAP Build Apps)
    • Automate processes (SAP Build Process Automation)
    • Build digital workspaces (SAP Build Work Zone)

All of this is done with drag-and-drop tooling and enterprise-grade governance. The main program flow looks as follows:

The GTT Route Simulator is launched via a simple form where the user selects a scenario and a departure date/time. At the moment there are three configured scenarios:

  • Guangzhou → Rotterdam (standard ocean route)
    • Guangzhou → Rotterdam with a delay in the Suez Canal
    • Rotterdam → Saarbrücken (on-carriage - road leg)

Based on the selection, the main flow chooses a path and calls a subprocess that reports the loading events. Opening the sub process for the departure and loading events, demonstrates the steps that are executed:

  • A small JavaScript snippet prepares the data that must be embedded in the XML message sent to GTT.
  • A predefined action then transmits the XML payload to the GTT tenant.
  • The subprocess proceeds to the next event, and when finished, returns to the main flow.

The next subprocess handles periodic GPS updates to create the illusion of the vessel moving across the ocean. Once Rotterdam is reached, arrival and unloading events are posted.

Exception Handling: The Delay Scenario

Transport is a business of exceptions, so the second scenario is identical to the first until the vessel reaches the Suez Canal. There, the simulator injects a 5-day delay event that leads to the delayed status of the Freight Booking. This same delay scenario is currently used quite often for demonstrating the Transportation Reschedule Agent—a powerful AI-driven rescheduling feature, also developed within the IT Plus scope.

Final-Mile Scenario

The third scenario covers on-carriage from Rotterdam to a warehouse in Saarbrücken. I intentionally built in a three-hour delay because, in many demos, colleagues (or me) manually trigger the final transport and warehouse events. If some of those events are skipped, the simulator ensures the road freight order still completes so we don’t leave “open” documents cluttering the demo system—a little clean-desk policy for our landscape.

Why a Simulator?

The GTT Route Simulator does exactly what its name suggests—it mimics the tracking information normally provided by an LSP or directly by an ocean carrier. While SAP Build could ultimately retrieve real events and forward them to GTT, in production you would usually choose an integration platform such as SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) for that task. The simulator is therefore a lightweight, rapid-prototyping tool that accelerates demos and proofs of concept.

entry.title: Re: The planned orders are being generated even though th...
entry.content:
Hello

As already mentioned, there is file attached to this question. 

The most common cause for this issue is the rescheduling check. Either your order is outside the rescheduling horizon, or it is not included in the rescheduling check. If this is the case, you will probably see exception message 20 for you production order in MD04.

You can take a look into the following wiki, which provides more details about the rescheduling check.

Best regards,
Caetano

entry.title: Re: SAP Business Data Cloud and Datasphere News in June
entry.content:
Enable System Notifications

I do not get the change here please. What is the difference to before the Notification Setting in user settings was introduced please?

I tested:

Task chain without any notification, setting OFF -> no email
Task chain with notification, setting OFF -> email arrives
Task chain without any notification, setting ON -> no email
Task chain with notification, setting ON -> email arrives

Seems no impact of the User Setting. Seems same impact 

I hoped I would get all failed notifications, even if my email is not setup as recipient. Something like this:

but no. My email still has to be in the recipients and I do not observe any differences with the switch on / off.

Thanks, Martin

@kpsauer
